data:
Organizational Diagnostic Report

Toyota Motor North America — Supply Chain & Procurement
Organizational Decision-Flow Diagnostic

Synthesis of 12 stakeholder interviews mapping decision ownership, information flows, KPI conflicts, and structural fault lines across the North American supply chain function.

Executive Summary

7
Contested Decisions
of 14 mapped decisions flagged
6
Active Fault Lines
cross-function & geography
7
KPI Conflict Pairs
systemic misalignment
14
N01 Degree
Michael Chen — anchor node
8
Opportunity Zones
structural gaps identified
18 mo
Shadow Forecast Age
unescalated by S&OP Lead
Critical Finding 1: The structural misalignment between cost-reduction KPIs (Finance & Procurement) and supply resilience investments (Supply Chain leadership) distorts decisions on semiconductor dual-sourcing, safety stock levels, and supplier development — and no single forum is equipped to resolve this. Hard cost metrics win by default against directional risk indicators every time.
Critical Finding 2: Georgetown (Plant SCM Marcus Thompson) exercises de facto sourcing authority bypassing formal procurement governance — tacitly endorsed by VP Manufacturing James Mueller but never formalized. Sarah Martinez documented a ~$400K annual pricing tier shortfall from Princeton plant non-compliance.
Critical Finding 3: The S&OP process is functionally compromised by a documented shadow forecasting practice that S&OP Process Lead Robert Park has acknowledged but declined to formally escalate for approximately 18 months, citing a belief that he does not own the problem.
Critical Finding 4: Decision ownership is ambiguous or contested across at least 7 high-stakes domains including semiconductor dual-sourcing rationalization, engineering change gate enforcement, supplier risk governance, and the logistics inclusion protocol — none has a single named owner with both authority and accountability.
Critical Finding 5: A governance gap exists between Michael Chen (VP SC) and the COO layer: multiple unresolved cross-functional decisions are stalled awaiting executive sponsorship that has not been formally requested. Rajesh Patel sent a formal resource request 10 days prior — awaiting response, with an October deadline at risk.
Priority Diagnostic Follow-Up Areas
Ranked by urgency and structural impact

Node size reflects connection degree. Michael Chen (N01) is the highest-degree node (14 edges), serving as the structural hub. Dr. Gregory Chen (N12) and Marcus Thompson (N05) are the most isolated nodes — Engineering has no direct edge to Finance or Logistics, and Georgetown has no direct edge to Supplier Development or Analytics. Drag nodes to reposition. Hover for details.

Decision Inventory

⚠ 7 of 14 mapped decisions are flagged as Contested or Ambiguous — concentrated in resilience investment, engineering-supply chain interface, demand forecasting governance, and plant-level exception authority.

⚠ flags appear on 7 of 14 decisions. The highest-risk contested domains are: resilience investment governance (dual-source rationalization, safety stock), plant-level exception authority (Georgetown sourcing bypass), and the engineering-supply chain interface (ECN gate, plan variance accountability). The ambiguous ownership of "total landed cost framework" is a particularly structural gap: the decision should be systematic but has no trigger, no owner, and no incentive reinforcement.

Meeting Map

13 recurring meetings catalogued. Classifications: Decision-Producing (outputs with named owners), Mixed (some decisions, some informational), Informational (awareness only), Performative (runs on cadence; no decisions produced), Defunct (previously active; no longer meeting).
MeetingFreq.Key AttendeesDecision OutputsTypeIssues
Executive S&OPMonthly Chen M. (chair), Park R., Morrison, Mueller, Lau, Okonkwo, Sales reps Production plan approval, inventory sign-off, forecast dispute escalation Mixed Overdense — forecast accountability avoided by chair
S&OP Demand ReviewMonthly Park R. (facilitator), Lim D. (planning), Chen S. (commercial) Demand forecast input to production plan Informational Underdense — shadow adjustments happen outside this forum
Monthly Commodity ReviewMonthly Martinez (chair), Okafor, Holt (risk issues), senior buyers Commodity strategy updates, watch-list flags, sourcing action triggers Mixed Medium-priority risks lack follow-through mechanism
Biweekly Production-Logistics TouchpointBiweekly Morrison, Lau (4–5 each side) Expedite requests, revised delivery windows, supplier escalations to procurement Decision-Producing No issues flagged — strong alignment between Morrison and Lau
Plant Operations Weekly (Georgetown)Weekly Thompson, Plant Manager, manufacturing leadership Supply-production alignment, supplier escalation flags Decision-Producing Plant-only; no Plano participation — island meeting
Daily Stand-Up (Georgetown)Daily Thompson, inbound logistics supervisors, line-side supervisors Expedite calls, safety stock draws, delivery exception handling Decision-Producing No issues flagged at tactical level
Executive Operations CouncilMonthly Park S. (COO, chair), Mueller, Chen M., Morrison, peers Resource allocation, cross-functional priority alignment, escalated disputes Mixed Underdense — SC-engineering tensions absent; no formal decision log cited
Plant Manager QuarterlyQuarterly Mueller (chair), all NA plant managers Operational performance, enterprise standards, best practice sharing Informational Underdense — exception sourcing protocol never formally addressed
Monthly Analytics ReviewMonthly Okonkwo (chair), supply chain function leads (variable attendance) Analytics KPI updates, model performance — awareness only Performative No decisions produced — does not connect to formal decision flow
Carrier Quarterly Business ReviewsQuarterly Lau (owner), carrier partners Carrier scorecards, contract adjustments, PIPs Decision-Producing No issues flagged
Program Management ReviewsMonthly Gregory Chen, program mgrs. (incl. Park D.), CPO Gate approvals/holds, late change log, schedule attainment Decision-Producing Engineering-supply chain notification gap not addressed in this forum
Design-for-Manufacturability SessionsQuarterly Gregory Chen, Huang R. (procurement rep), manufacturing rep Design simplification, tooling decisions — quality variable Mixed Underdense — SC strategic participation inconsistent; below-seniority procurement rep
Supply Resilience Working GroupDefunct Chen M. (chair), Okafor, logistics, manufacturing, finance (observer) Supplier risk profiles, scenario modeling — now inactive Defunct Lost momentum — was decision-producing; disbanded post-shortage normalization. No replacement.
The Executive S&OP and Monthly Commodity Review are the two highest-stakes meetings but are both structurally compromised. The Monthly Analytics Review runs on cadence but is purely performative — no decision trail connects it to operations. The Supply Resilience Working Group being defunct without a standing replacement is the single largest meeting-structure gap: its risk artifacts (single-point-of-failure maps, Taiwan Strait scenarios) have no current custodian.

KPI Conflict Analysis

The most systemic pattern: hard cost KPIs vs. soft directional risk indicators. In every governance conversation, numeric cost targets (PPV, cost realization, inventory carrying %) structurally defeat composite risk scores and resilience metrics. This asymmetry is not a judgment failure — it is a measurement design problem.
Rajesh Patel (Finance)
Inventory Carrying Cost <18% of procurement spend
⇄
Michael Chen (VP SC)
Resilience Score: dual-source %, safety stock coverage
Reducing safety stock and consolidating to single-source improves working capital but degrades resilience. Optimizing resilience inflates carrying cost (currently tracking 22–23% vs. 18% target). The asymmetry — Patel has a hard target number, Chen has a directional composite — means Patel wins every budget conversation by default.
Severity
HIGH
Dual-Source RationalizationSafety Stock Level-Setting2025 Operating Plan
Sarah Martinez (Procurement)
PPV: 3% YoY reduction on direct materials
⇄
Patricia Lau (Logistics)
Freight Cost <5% of procurement spend
Procurement's unit cost optimization (supplier consolidation, offshore sourcing) increases freight complexity and expedite premiums, degrading Lau's metrics without appearing in Martinez's scorecard. Lau's expedite rate is currently 3.2% against a 2.5% target — partially attributable to sourcing decisions made without logistics input.
Severity
HIGH
Supplier Location DecisionsLogistics Inclusion in Sourcing
Rajesh Patel (Finance)
Procurement Cost Realization: 90% of committed savings (tracking 74–76%)
⇄
David Okafor (Commodity Mgr.)
Supply Risk Score: composite directional indicator (no hard target)
Dual-sourcing premium compresses realized savings; Okafor's risk justification carries no hard dollar target and structurally loses to a numeric realization shortfall. Okafor described responding to pressure he receives "secondhand rather than making his case directly" — he has no direct relationship with Finance.
Severity
HIGH
Dual-Source Contract FlexibilityVolume Exclusivity Trade-offs
Robert Park (S&OP Lead)
Forecast Accuracy: ±10% at model level (monthly)
⇄
Tom Bradley (VP Sales)
No formal forecast accuracy KPI on commercial side
Supply chain carries accountability for a metric substantially driven by commercial inputs that commercial leadership is not measured on. This asymmetry structurally enables the shadow forecasting practice: planners correct commercial over-forecasts informally because there is no formal mechanism to assign correction accountability upward.
Severity
HIGH
Demand Review CredibilityShadow ForecastingProduction Plan Variance
Lisa Morrison (Dir. Production Planning)
Production Plan Variance <3% month-over-month
⇄
Dr. Gregory Chen (Dir. Engineering)
Program Schedule Attainment: 90% gate attainment
Engineering's gate attainment metric does not penalize late changes occurring after design freeze. Changes that slip post-freeze create plan variance for Morrison (breaching her <3% target) but register as on-time for Chen's scorecard. The seating bracket ECN retrofit caused hundreds of thousands in cost — visible in Morrison's variance, invisible in Chen's gate metric.
Severity
HIGH
ECN Gate EnforcementPlan Variance Accountability
James Mueller (VP Manufacturing)
OEE ≥95% / Zero Line Stoppages
⇄
Michael Chen (VP SC)
On-time Delivery ≥98% / Total Landed Cost –2%
Mueller's OEE mandate creates pressure for exception local sourcing that violates enterprise supplier agreements, undermining Chen's cost and compliance targets. The Georgetown precedent was explicitly framed by Mueller as "a manufacturing standpoint judgment" — his KPI architecture incentivizes exactly this behavior.
Severity
MEDIUM
Exception Sourcing ProtocolEnterprise Agreement Compliance
Amara Okonkwo (Analytics)
Forecast MAPE improvement: –15pp vs. commercial forecast ✓ (hitting target)
⇄
Robert Park (S&OP Lead)
S&OP Process Compliance: 95% (meetings on cadence, inputs on time)
Okonkwo's model outperforms the commercial forecast (her KPI is green) but planners informally use shadow adjustments rather than formally adopting her model output, creating a compliance gap that Robert cannot surface without exposing the shadow forecasting practice he has chosen not to escalate.
Severity
MEDIUM
Demand Sensing AdoptionS&OP Process Integrity

Fault Line Analysis

Six structural fault lines identified across 12 interviews. Prevalence counts indicate how many of 12 interviews surfaced each fault line — higher prevalence = more systemic, not merely dyadic.
⚡
FL-1: Plant Operations vs. Corporate Procurement
Surfaced in 6 of 12 interviews — most prevalent fault line
HIGH

Marcus Thompson exercises de facto sourcing authority that bypasses formal procurement governance. Sarah Martinez lost enforcement of an enterprise fastener agreement at Princeton plant, resulting in ~$400K annual pricing tier shortfall unresolved. The fault line is structurally enabled by Mueller's OEE KPI incentivizing plant-level workarounds.

"What I own outright... expedited or emergency local sourcing when the schedule is at risk. That last one is technically in a gray zone formally, but operationally I own it because I'm the one who has to keep the plant running." — Marcus Thompson
"Plano's model is built on a lean inventory assumption that looks good on paper... but Georgetown's reality has variability that the model doesn't fully capture." — Marcus Thompson
Emergency Exception SourcingEnterprise Agreement CompliancePlant Safety StockSupplier Qualification Bypass
💰
FL-2: Finance vs. Supply Chain on Resilience Investment
Surfaced in 7 of 12 interviews — most widely distributed fault line
HIGH

Post-COVID resilience investments (dual sourcing, safety stock) are being challenged by Finance as cost inefficiencies now that the shortage emergency has passed. The 2025 dual-source decision framework has no analytical resource assigned and an October deadline at risk. Patel sent a formal email request to Chen 10 days prior — no response received.

"The CFO's chief of staff pushed back... if lead times had normalized, the risk justification for the slow drawdown wasn't there." — Rajesh Patel
"[The supply risk score] doesn't carry the same weight as a cost number." — David Okafor
Dual-Source RationalizationSafety Stock Level-Setting2025 Operating PlanResilience Working Group
🌐
FL-3: HQ Japan vs. North America Decision Rights
Surfaced in 4 of 12 interviews
HIGH

Informal HQ influence shapes North American decisions before they become formal proposals. The dual-sourcing expansion was walked back after a bilateral call with the HQ VP of Supply Chain — the block was never put in writing. North American ownership of strategic sourcing decisions is real on paper and illusory in practice for decisions above a certain scale threshold.

"I've had decisions significantly shaped by anticipated HQ reaction before they ever became formal proposals." — Michael Chen
"Nobody puts a block in writing if they can avoid it — you have a conversation, the message lands, and the decision adjusts." — Michael Chen
Dual-Source Investment ScaleSafety Stock PhilosophySupplier Qualification ThresholdsQBR Framing
